title: Emergency First Aid: Cat Laceration with Active Bleeding
meta_desc: Active bleeding in cats is an emergency. Learn how to manage lacerations, the importance of triage photos, and why immediate vet care is vital.
content_html:
Understanding Your Cat's Laceration

A laceration is a deep cut or tear in the skin and underlying soft tissues. In cats, these are often caused by sharp objects, environmental hazards, or animal bites. Because feline skin is quite thin, even a seemingly small tear can expose muscle or connective tissue, leading to significant blood loss and a high risk of infection.

Urgency Level: High (Emergency)

Visible lacerations with active bleeding are considered a high-priority emergency. If blood is pulsing, soaking through bandages, or failing to stop after five minutes of direct pressure, your cat requires immediate veterinary intervention. Rapid blood loss can lead to shock, while open wounds are highly susceptible to bacteria that cause painful abscesses.

The Importance of Triage Photos

When contacting your veterinarian, providing a clear photo of the wound can be life-saving. A visual assessment allows the triage team to determine the depth of the injury, identify if an artery is involved, and estimate the need for immediate surgery. This preparation ensures that the medical team is ready to stabilize your pet the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: What should I do first to help my cat?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, continuous pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or sterile gauze. Do not remove the cloth if it becomes soaked; simply place another layer on top to avoid disturbing any clots.

faqs.question: Should I clean the wound with hydrogen peroxide?
faqs.answer: No. Hydrogen peroxide can damage the healthy tissue and delay the healing process. It is best to keep the wound covered and let a veterinarian clean it using sterile instruments and solutions.

faqs.question: Will my cat need stitches?
faqs.answer: Most active-bleeding lacerations require sutures (stitches) or surgical staples to close the gap, prevent infection, and ensure the skin heals correctly without leaving a large, vulnerable scar.
